subject: Physics
q: In a Young's double slit experiment, a laser light of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>560</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> produces an interference pattern with consecutive bright fringes' separation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. Now another light is used to produce an interference pattern with consecutive bright fringes' separation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The wavelength of second light is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: In a Young's double slit experiment, a laser light of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>560</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> produces an interference pattern with consecutive bright fringes' separation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. Now another light is used to produce an interference pattern with consecutive bright fringes' separation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The wavelength of second light is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Given here: λ1=560×10-9 m, β1=7.2×10-3 m and β2=8.1×10-3 m.

Let d be the width and D be the distance between slit and screen then fringe width, β=λDd.

Thus, from above,  β∝λ

So, the wavelength of second light is λ2=β2β1λ1=98λ1

∴   λ2=98×560=630 nm.

subject: Physics
q: The angular width of the central maximum in a single slit diffraction pattern is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> . The width of the slit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μm</mi></math>. The slit is illuminated by monochromatic plane waves. If another slit of the same width is made near it, Young's fringes can be observed on a screen placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>cm</mi></math> from the slits. If the observed fringe width is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>cm</mi></math>, what is slit separation distance? (i.e., the distance between the centres of each slit.)
question: The angular width of the central maximum in a single slit diffraction pattern is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> . The width of the slit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μm</mi></math>. The slit is illuminated by monochromatic plane waves. If another slit of the same width is made near it, Young's fringes can be observed on a screen placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>cm</mi></math> from the slits. If the observed fringe width is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>cm</mi></math>, what is slit separation distance? (i.e., the distance between the centres of each slit.)
solution:
In diffraction, dsin30°=λ, λ=d2



Young’s fringe width [ d'- the separation between two slits] 

β=λ×Dd'⇒10-2=d2×50×10-2d'
⇒10-2=10-6×50×10-22×d'⇒d'=25 μm.

subject: Physics
q: An unpolarized light beam is incident on the polarizer of a polarization experiment and the intensity of light beam emerging from the analyzer is measured as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> Lumens. Now, if the analyzer is rotated around the horizontal axis (direction of light) by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> in clockwise direction, the intensity of emerging light will be _______Lumens.
question: An unpolarized light beam is incident on the polarizer of a polarization experiment and the intensity of light beam emerging from the analyzer is measured as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> Lumens. Now, if the analyzer is rotated around the horizontal axis (direction of light) by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> in clockwise direction, the intensity of emerging light will be _______Lumens.
solution:
Assuming initially axis of Polarizer and Analyzer are parallel

Now emerging intensity =I02cos230°

=100322=100×34=75

subject: Physics
q: A parallel beam of electrons travelling in x - direction falls on a slit of width d (see figure). If after passing the slit, an electron acquires momentum py in the y - direction then for a majority of electrons passing through the slit (h is Planck's constant):
question: A parallel beam of electrons travelling in x - direction falls on a slit of width d (see figure). If after passing the slit, an electron acquires momentum py in the y - direction then for a majority of electrons passing through the slit (h is Planck's constant):
solution:
The electron beam will be diffractive at an angle θ



For Ist minima

dsin⁡θ= λ

dsin⁡θ= hp

Also psin⁡θ=py

⇒    d py=h

∴ majority of electrons passing through the slit are lying in the central maxima d py <h

subject: Physics
q: Two monochromatic light beams have intensities in the ratio 1:9. An interference pattern is obtained by these beams. The ratio of the intensities of maximum to minimum is
question: Two monochromatic light beams have intensities in the ratio 1:9. An interference pattern is obtained by these beams. The ratio of the intensities of maximum to minimum is
solution: $\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{\left(\sqrt{I_1}+\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2}{\left(\sqrt{I_1}-\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2} \Rightarrow \frac{(4)^2}{(2)^2} \Rightarrow \frac{16}{4}=4$

subject: Physics
q: A single slit of width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is illuminated by a monochromatic light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> for which first minimum appears at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>30</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup></math> on the screen will be :
question: A single slit of width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is illuminated by a monochromatic light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> for which first minimum appears at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>30</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup></math> on the screen will be :
solution:
For the first minima

a sinθ=λ 

The given data is 

θ=30°λ=600 nm

The value of a is 

a=λsin30°=600×10-912=1.2 μm

q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : In Young's double slit experiment, the fringes produced by red light are closer as compared to those produced by blue light.
Reason (R): The fringe width is directly proportional to the wavelength of light.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : In Young's double slit experiment, the fringes produced by red light are closer as compared to those produced by blue light.
Reason (R): The fringe width is directly proportional to the wavelength of light.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both $(\mathbf{A})$ and $(\mathbf{R})$ are true but $(\mathbf{R})$ is NOT the correct explanation of $(\mathbf{A})$
Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)

subject: Physics
q: A beam of plane polarized light of large cross-sectional area and uniform intensity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.3</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> falls normally on a polarizer (cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>), which rotates about its axis with an angular speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>31</mn><mo>.4</mo><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The energy of light passing through the polarizer per revolution, is close to:
question: A beam of plane polarized light of large cross-sectional area and uniform intensity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.3</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> falls normally on a polarizer (cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>), which rotates about its axis with an angular speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>31</mn><mo>.4</mo><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The energy of light passing through the polarizer per revolution, is close to:
solution:
Given,

Intensity of plane polarized light of large cross-sectional area is I0=3.3 W m-2

Area of cross-section of polarizer is A=3×10-4 m2

Angular speed of rotation is ω=31.4 rad s-1

The intensity of light is the given by the definition,

 I=EAT  ...(1)

Intensity of light passing through the polarizer as polarizer rotates is given by

Iavg=I01T∫0Tcos2ωtdt

⇒Iavg=I01T∫0T1+cos2ωt2dt

⇒Iavg=I012T∫0Tdt+∫0Tcos2ωtdt

⇒Iavg=I012TT+0

⇒Iavg=I02  ...(2)

Using equation (1) and (2) we get energy per revolution is given by

E=I0A2T=I0A22πω

E=3.3×3×10−423.1431.4

E≃1×10-4 J

subject: Physics
q: A double slit interference experiment performed with a light of wavelength 600 nm forms an interference fringe pattern on a screen with 10 th bright fringe having its centre at a distance of 10 mm from the central maximum. Distance of the centre of the same 10th bright fringe from the central maximum when the source of light is replaced by another source of wavelength 660 nm would be _________ mm .
question: A double slit interference experiment performed with a light of wavelength 600 nm forms an interference fringe pattern on a screen with 10 th bright fringe having its centre at a distance of 10 mm from the central maximum. Distance of the centre of the same 10th bright fringe from the central maximum when the source of light is replaced by another source of wavelength 660 nm would be _________ mm .
solution:
Position of the $n^{\text {th }}$ bright fringe w.r.t. central maxima
in a YDSE is $y_n=n \frac{\lambda D}{d}$.
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{y_{10}^{\prime}}{y_{10}}=\frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}$
or $\quad y_{10}^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda} y_{10}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left(\frac{660 \mathrm{~nm}}{600 \mathrm{~nm}}\right) 10 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & =11 \mathrm{~mm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: As shown in figures, three identical polaroids P1, P2 and P3 are placed one after another. The pass axis of P2 and P3 are inclined at angle of 60º and 90º with respect to axis of P1. The source S has an intensity of 256 W m-2. The intensity of light at point O is _____W m-2.
question: As shown in figures, three identical polaroids P1, P2 and P3 are placed one after another. The pass axis of P2 and P3 are inclined at angle of 60º and 90º with respect to axis of P1. The source S has an intensity of 256 W m-2. The intensity of light at point O is _____W m-2.
solution:
As given here, the pass axis of P2 and P3 are inclined at angle of 60º and 90º with respect to axis of P1. 

Therefore, intensity of light after falling on first polaroid P1, I1=I02

Intensity of light after falling on second polaroid P2, I2=I1cos260°=I08

Intensity of light after falling on third polaroid P3, I3=I2cos230°=I0834=3I032

The intensity of light at O is 3I032=3×25632=24 W m-2

subject: Physics
q: In a Young's double slit experiment with light of wavelength λ, the separation of slits is d and distance of screen is D such that D≫d≫λ . If the Fringe width is β , the distance from point of maximum intensity to the point where intensity falls to half of the maximum intensity on either side is:
question: In a Young's double slit experiment with light of wavelength λ, the separation of slits is d and distance of screen is D such that D≫d≫λ . If the Fringe width is β , the distance from point of maximum intensity to the point where intensity falls to half of the maximum intensity on either side is:
solution:
As Imax=4I

Then Inet=12 Imax=2I

⇒2I=2I1+cosϕ

⇒1+cosϕ=1

⇒cosϕ=0

⇒ϕ=π2,3π2,5π2, …

 ϕ=2πλΔx

⇒π2=2πλΔx

⇒Δx=λ4,3λ4,5λ4, …




Also path difference Δx=dsin⁡θ =λ4

At desired location

⇒d ynD=λ4

⇒ yn=λD4d=β4 ∵β=λDd

⇒ Separation between central maxima and desired y=β4

subject: Physics
q: In Young's double slit experiment, the fringe width of the interference pattern produced on the screen is $2.4$ $\mu$m. If the experiment is carried out in another medium having refractive index $1.2$, the fringe width will be _____ $\mu$m.
question: In Young's double slit experiment, the fringe width of the interference pattern produced on the screen is $2.4$ $\mu$m. If the experiment is carried out in another medium having refractive index $1.2$, the fringe width will be _____ $\mu$m.
solution:
The fringe width in Young's double slit experiment is given by $\beta = \dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$.

When the experiment is performed in a medium of refractive index $\mu$, the wavelength of light becomes $\lambda' = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$.

The new fringe width is $\beta' = \dfrac{\lambda' D}{d} = \dfrac{\lambda D}{\mu d} = \dfrac{\beta}{\mu}$.

Substituting the given values, $\beta = 2.4$ $\mu$m and $\mu = 1.2$:

$\beta' = \dfrac{2.4}{1.2} = 2$ $\mu$m.

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
q: In a single slit experiment, a parallel beam of green light of wavelength $550 \mathrm{~nm}$ passes through a slit of width $0.20 \mathrm{~mm}$. The transmitted light is collected on a screen $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ away. The distance of first order minima from the central maximum will be $x \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}$. The value of $x$ is :
question: In a single slit experiment, a parallel beam of green light of wavelength $550 \mathrm{~nm}$ passes through a slit of width $0.20 \mathrm{~mm}$. The transmitted light is collected on a screen $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ away. The distance of first order minima from the central maximum will be $x \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}$. The value of $x$ is :
solution: $\mathrm{y}=\frac{\lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{550 \times 10^{-9} \times 100 \times 10^{-2}}{0.2 \times 10^{-3}}=275$

In the experiment, a source of light of wavelength <math><mn>589</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is used and the interference pattern is observed on a screen kept <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away. The separation between the successive bright fringes on the screen is:
question: In a Young’s double slit experiment, the separation between the slits is <math><mn>0.15</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> . In the experiment, a source of light of wavelength <math><mn>589</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is used and the interference pattern is observed on a screen kept <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away. The separation between the successive bright fringes on the screen is:
solution: β=λDd=589×10-9×1.50.15×10-3=5.9mm

q: A beam of unpolarised light of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is passed through a polaroid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and then through another polaroid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> which is oriented so that its principal plane makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> relative to that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. The intensity of emergent light is :
question: A beam of unpolarised light of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is passed through a polaroid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and then through another polaroid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> which is oriented so that its principal plane makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> relative to that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. The intensity of emergent light is :
solution:
Intensity of polarised light from polaroid A will be I02. Therefore,

Intensity of emergent light
=I02cos245°=I04

q: Interference fringes are observed on a screen by illuminating two thin slits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> apart with a light source <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>632</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. question: Interference fringes are observed on a screen by illuminating two thin slits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> apart with a light source <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>632</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. solution:
ΔP=dsinθ

=dθ

=dyD=10-3×1.270 mm1 m=1.27μm

1.27μm

q: A fringe width of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> was produced for two slits separated by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> apart. The screen is placed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away. The wavelength of light used is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> to the nearest integer is ______.
question: A fringe width of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> was produced for two slits separated by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> apart. The screen is placed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away. The wavelength of light used is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> to the nearest integer is ______.
solution:
β=λDd

λ=βdD

λ=6×10-3×10-310

λ=6×10-7 m=600×10-9 m

λ=600 nm

subject: Physics
q: The Young's double slit interference experiment is performed using light consisting of 480 nm and 600 nm wavelengths to form interference patterns. The least number of the bright fringes of 480 nm light that are required for the first coincidence with the bright fringes formed by 600 nm light is
question: The Young's double slit interference experiment is performed using light consisting of 480 nm and 600 nm wavelengths to form interference patterns. The least number of the bright fringes of 480 nm light that are required for the first coincidence with the bright fringes formed by 600 nm light is
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{n_1 \lambda_1 D}{d}=\frac{n_2 \lambda_2 D}{d} \\
& n 480=m 600 \\
& n_{\min }=5
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A telescope with objective diameter $R$ is used to observe a distant star emitting light of wavelength $500$ nm, at a resolution of $5 \times 10^{-7}$ radian. The value of $R$ is _____ cm.
question: A telescope with objective diameter $R$ is used to observe a distant star emitting light of wavelength $500$ nm, at a resolution of $5 \times 10^{-7}$ radian. The value of $R$ is _____ cm.
solution:
The limit of resolution of a telescope is given by the formula:

$\Delta \theta = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{R}$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light and $R$ is the diameter of the objective lens.

Given:
$\lambda = 500 \text{ nm} = 500 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$
$\Delta \theta = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ rad}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$5 \times 10^{-7} = \dfrac{1.22 \times 5 \times 10^{-7}}{R}$

$R = 1.22 \text{ m}$

Converting to centimeters:

$R = 1.22 \times 100 \text{ cm} = 122 \text{ cm}$

Answer: $122$

subject: Physics
q: In the Young's double slit experiment, the distance between the slits varies in time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math>; where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> are constants. The difference between the largest fringe width and the smallest fringe width obtained over time is given as:
question: In the Young's double slit experiment, the distance between the slits varies in time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math>; where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> are constants. The difference between the largest fringe width and the smallest fringe width obtained over time is given as:
solution:
Fringe width, 

β=λDd

βmax⇒dmin and βmin⇒dmax

d=d0+a0sinωt

dmax=d0+a0 and dmin=d0-a0

∴ βmin=λDd0+a0 and ∴ βmax=λDd0-a0

βmax-βmin=λDd0-a0-λDd0+a0=2λDa0 d02-a02

subject: Physics
q: An unpolarized light is incident on the plane interface of air-dielectric medium shown in figure. If the incident angle is equal to Brewster angle, identify the expression representing reflected wave.
question: An unpolarized light is incident on the plane interface of air-dielectric medium shown in figure. If the incident angle is equal to Brewster angle, identify the expression representing reflected wave.
solution:
From the given figure, the incident light rays lie in the $xz$-plane. Therefore, the plane of incidence is the $xz$-plane.

The incident wave travels from the air ($z < 0$) towards the medium ($z > 0$), so it propagates in the $+x$ and $+z$ directions. The reflected wave will travel back into the air, meaning it propagates in the $+x$ and $-z$ directions.

The wave vector for the reflected wave can be written as $\vec{k}_r = k_x \hat{i} - k_z \hat{k}$.
The phase of the reflected wave is given by $\vec{k}_r \cdot \vec{r} - \omega t = k_x x - k_z z - \omega t$. This phase matches the expression in option (1).

According to Brewster's law, when unpolarized light is incident at the Brewster angle, the reflected light is completely polarized perpendicular to the plane of incidence. Since the plane of incidence is the $xz$-plane, the reflected electric field must be entirely along the $y$-axis ($\hat{j}$ direction).

Let us verify this with option (1), which gives the electric field as $\vec{E} = E_x \hat{i} + E_y \hat{j}$.
For an electromagnetic wave, the electric field must be perpendicular to the direction of propagation, so $\vec{k}_r \cdot \vec{E} = 0$.
$(k_x \hat{i} - k_z \hat{k}) \cdot (E_x \hat{i} + E_y \hat{j}) = 0$
$k_x E_x = 0$

Since $k_x \neq 0$, we must have $E_x = 0$. This leaves $\vec{E} = E_y \hat{j}$, which means the wave is purely polarized along the $y$-axis. This perfectly satisfies the condition for reflection at the Brewster angle.

Thus, the correct expression representing the reflected wave is $(E_x\hat{i} + E_y\hat{j})\sin(kx - kz - \omega t)$.

Answer: $(E_x\hat{i} + E_y\hat{j})\sin(kx - kz - \omega t)$

subject: Physics
q: The figure shows a Young's double slit experimental setup. It is observed that when a thin transparent sheet of thickness t and refractive index μ is put in front of one of the slits, the central maximum gets shifted by a distance equal to n fringe width. If the wavelength of light used is λ then t will be:
question: The figure shows a Young's double slit experimental setup. It is observed that when a thin transparent sheet of thickness t and refractive index μ is put in front of one of the slits, the central maximum gets shifted by a distance equal to n fringe width. If the wavelength of light used is λ then t will be:
solution:
Shift in central maxima =Daμ-1t
Fringe width =Dλa
It's given,
Daμ-1t=nDλa
t=nλμ-1

subject: Physics
q: If the measured angular separation between the second minimum to the left of the central maximum and the third minimum to the right of the central maximum is $30^{\circ}$ in a single slit diffraction pattern recorded using 628 nm light, then the width of the slit is _______ $\mu \mathrm{m}$.
question: If the measured angular separation between the second minimum to the left of the central maximum and the third minimum to the right of the central maximum is $30^{\circ}$ in a single slit diffraction pattern recorded using 628 nm light, then the width of the slit is _______ $\mu \mathrm{m}$.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \theta_1=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \lambda}{a}\right) \\ & \theta_2=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 \lambda}{a}\right) \\ & \because \quad \theta_1+\theta_2=30^{\circ}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}}\right)=\frac{\pi}{6} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}} \sqrt{1-\left(\frac{3 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}}\right)^2}+\frac{3 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}} \sqrt{1+\left(\frac{2 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}}\right)^2}=\sin \frac{\pi}{6}\end{aligned}$
Here $\lambda=628 \mathrm{~nm}$
After solving
$\mathrm{A}=6.07 \mu \mathrm{~m}$
Approximate Method :
$\begin{aligned} & \theta=\theta_1+\theta_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}+\frac{3 \lambda}{a} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{5}{a}(628 \mathrm{~nm}) \\ & \Rightarrow a=6 \mu \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: In a Young's double slit experiment, three polarizers are kept as shown in the figure. The transmission axes of $P_1$ and $P_2$ are orthogonal to each other. The polarizer $P_3$ covers both the slits with its transmission axis at $45^{\circ}$ to those of $P_1$ and $P_2$. An unpolarized light of wavelength $\lambda$ and intensity $I_0$ is incident on $P_1$ and $P_2$. The intensity at a point after $P_3$ where the path difference between the light waves from $s_1$ and $s_2$ is $\frac{\lambda}{3}$, is
question: In a Young's double slit experiment, three polarizers are kept as shown in the figure. The transmission axes of $P_1$ and $P_2$ are orthogonal to each other. The polarizer $P_3$ covers both the slits with its transmission axis at $45^{\circ}$ to those of $P_1$ and $P_2$. An unpolarized light of wavelength $\lambda$ and intensity $I_0$ is incident on $P_1$ and $P_2$. The intensity at a point after $P_3$ where the path difference between the light waves from $s_1$ and $s_2$ is $\frac{\lambda}{3}$, is
solution: after passing through third poleriser, Intensity of both the waves must be $\frac{\mathrm{I}_0}{4}$ now, at a point where path diff is $\frac{\lambda}{3}$, phase difference $\begin{aligned} & \Delta \phi=2 \mathrm{~K}\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{x}}{\lambda}\right)=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{res}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mathrm{I}_0}{4}\right)^2+\left(\frac{\mathrm{I}_0}{4}\right)^2+2\left(\frac{\mathrm{I}_0}{4}\right)^2} \cos \frac{2 \pi}{3} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{I}_0}{4} \end{aligned}$

q: In a double – slit experiment, at a certain point on the screen the path difference between the two interfering waves is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> of a wavelength. The ratio of the intensity of light at that point to that at the center of a bright fringe is:
question: In a double – slit experiment, at a certain point on the screen the path difference between the two interfering waves is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> of a wavelength. The ratio of the intensity of light at that point to that at the center of a bright fringe is:
solution:
I=I0cos2∆ϕ2
II0=cos22πλ×∆X2=cos2π8;II0=0.853

subject: Physics
q: Young's double slit inteference apparatus is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.44. It has slit separation of 1.5 mm . The slits are illuminated by a parallel beam of light whose wavelength in air is 690 nm . The fringe-width on a screen placed behind the plane of slits at a distance of 0.72 m , will be :
question: Young's double slit inteference apparatus is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.44. It has slit separation of 1.5 mm . The slits are illuminated by a parallel beam of light whose wavelength in air is 690 nm . The fringe-width on a screen placed behind the plane of slits at a distance of 0.72 m , will be :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta W=\frac{\lambda D}{d \cdot \mu}=\frac{690 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.72}{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.44} \\ & \Rightarrow \Delta W=2.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}=0.23 \mathrm{~mm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: White light is passed through a double slit and interference is observed on a screen <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away. The separation between the slits is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The first violet and red fringes are formed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> away from the central white fringes. The difference in wavelengths of red and violet light is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mtext>in</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math>
question: White light is passed through a double slit and interference is observed on a screen <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away. The separation between the slits is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The first violet and red fringes are formed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> away from the central white fringes. The difference in wavelengths of red and violet light is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mtext>in</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
y1=λrDd

y2=λvDd

λr−λv=y1−y2dD=(3.5−2)mm×0.3mm1.5m=300nm

q: In a Young's double slit experiment <math><mn>15</mn></math> fringes are observed on a small portion of the screen when light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is used. Ten fringes are observed on the same section of the screen when another light source of wavelength <math><mi>λ</mi></math> is used. Then the value of <math><mi>λ</mi></math> is (in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> ) __________.
question: In a Young's double slit experiment <math><mn>15</mn></math> fringes are observed on a small portion of the screen when light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is used. Ten fringes are observed on the same section of the screen when another light source of wavelength <math><mi>λ</mi></math> is used. Then the value of <math><mi>λ</mi></math> is (in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> ) __________.
solution:
15×500×Dd=10×λ2×Dd
λ2=15×50 nm
λ2=750 nm

subject: Physics
q: Sodium light of wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>650</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>655</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> is used to study diffraction at a single slit of aperture <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The distance between the slit and the screen is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The separation between the positions of the first maxima of diffraction pattern obtained in the two cases is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>
question: Sodium light of wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>650</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>655</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> is used to study diffraction at a single slit of aperture <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The distance between the slit and the screen is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The separation between the positions of the first maxima of diffraction pattern obtained in the two cases is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>
solution:
The condition for maxima in diffraction is,

bsinθ=2n+1λ2.

 For the first maxima n=1. Therefore,

bsinθ=3λ2⇒sinθ=3λ2b=yDy=3λD2b

Now the difference between the positions of the maxima will be,

y1-y2=3D2bλ1-λ2⇒y1-y2=3×22×5×10-4×655-650×10-9=3×10-5 m

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : The beam of electrons shows wave nature and exhibit interference and diffraction.

Reason R : Davisson Germer Experimentally verified the wave nature of electrons.

In the light of the above statements. Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements: One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : The beam of electrons shows wave nature and exhibit interference and diffraction.

Reason R : Davisson Germer Experimentally verified the wave nature of electrons.

In the light of the above statements. Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: A is correct but R is not correct
A is not correct but R is correct
Both A and R are correct but R is Not the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
solution:
The Davisson-Germer experiment showed that electron beams can undergo diffraction when passed through the atomic crystals. This shows that the wave nature of electrons. When electron is in wave nature it can exhibit interference and show diffraction.

Hence, Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A.
